summary: Jeff Atwood profiles Steve Mann, an engineering professor who has lived as a cyborg with wearable computers since 1980. Mann wears a web of wires, computers, and sensors designed to augment memory, enhance vision, and monitor vital signs. Atwood notes that despite Mann's pioneering work spanning over two decades and reaching 'version 7' of his wearable rig, the field seems to have stagnated. The post observes that most wearable computing resources, including Mann's own website and MIT's wearable computing page, appear neglected or abandoned. Atwood finds this ironic given the futuristic nature of the technology.
key_points: Steve Mann has been a pioneer in wearable computing since 1980, wearing computer systems that augment his human capabilities
Mann's work gained mainstream attention around 2001 with a book and documentary, but appears to have plateaued since
The wearable computing field's web presence is surprisingly neglected, with most major resources not updated since the mid-2000s
Despite being on 'version 7' of his rig, Mann hasn't visibly updated since 1999
money_quotes: Steve Mann may be the world's original cyborg.

Steve Mann, an engineering professor at the University of Toronto, has lived as a cyborg for more than 20 years, wearing a web of wires, computers and electronic sensors that are designed to augment his memory, enhance his vision and keep tabs on his vital signs.
Somehow, I thought the cyborgs would have a more compelling web presence.
key_insight: The pioneers of wearable computing, despite their futuristic vision, have ironically failed to maintain a compelling presence in the digital world they helped imagine.
